stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]x + [[c]] \). Since \( f'(x) = 2([[a]])x + [[b]] \) changes sign from negative to positive at its unique zero, \( f \) has an absolute minimum there. What is the minimum value of \( f \)?
solution: The critical point is at \( x=-\dfrac{[[b]]}{2\cdot[[a]]} \) (where \( f'(x)=0 \)). Substituting back into \( f \) gives the minimum value \( f\!\left(-\dfrac{[[b]]}{2\cdot[[a]]}\right) = [[c]] - \dfrac{[[b]]^{2}}{4\cdot[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( f(0) \), not the minimum value at the vertex.
This is the \( x \)-coordinate of the minimum, not the minimum VALUE.
This has the wrong sign on the vertex correction term.
This has the correct terms but the wrong overall sign.
This is off by one from the correct minimum value.
This is off by one from the correct minimum value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{2} + [[b]]x + [[c]] \). Since \( f'(x) = 2([[a]])x + [[b]] \) changes sign from positive to negative at its unique zero, \( f \) has an absolute maximum there. What is the maximum value of \( f \)?
solution: The critical point is at \( x=-\dfrac{[[b]]}{2\cdot[[a]]} \) (where \( f'(x)=0 \)). Substituting back into \( f \) gives the maximum value \( f\!\left(-\dfrac{[[b]]}{2\cdot[[a]]}\right) = [[c]] - \dfrac{[[b]]^{2}}{4\cdot[[a]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( f(0) \), not the maximum value at the vertex.
This is the \( x \)-coordinate of the maximum, not the maximum VALUE.
This has the wrong sign on the vertex correction term.
This has the correct terms but the wrong overall sign.
This is off by one from the correct maximum value.
This is off by one from the correct maximum value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{3} + ([[B]])x^{2} + ([[C]])x + ([[d]]) \). Its derivative \( f'(x) = 3([[a]])(x-[[r1]])(x-[[r2]]) \) is zero at \( x=[[r1]] \) and \( x=[[r2]] \), giving \( f \) a local maximum and a local minimum. Using the first derivative test, find the local MINIMUM value of \( f \).
solution: Since \( [[a]]>0 \) and \( [[r1]]<[[r2]] \), \( f \) decreases on \( ([[r1]],[[r2]]) \) and increases outside it, so the local minimum is at \( x=[[r2]] \). Evaluating, \( f([[r2]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the local MAXIMUM value (at \( x=[[r1]] \)), not the minimum.
This is \( f(0) \), the constant term, not the local minimum value.
This is the \( x \)-location of the minimum, not the minimum VALUE.
This has the wrong sign on the constant term \( [[d]] \).
This is off by two from the correct minimum value.
This is off by two from the correct minimum value.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{3} + ([[B]])x^{2} + ([[C]])x + ([[d]]) \), which has a local maximum at \( x=[[r1]] \) and a local minimum at \( x=[[r2]] \). What is the SUM of the local maximum value and the local minimum value of \( f \)?
solution: The local maximum value is \( f([[r1]]) \) and the local minimum value is \( f([[r2]]) \). Adding them gives \( f([[r1]]) + f([[r2]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is only the local maximum value, not the sum of both extrema.
This is only the local minimum value, not the sum of both extrema.
This is the DIFFERENCE of the two extreme values, not their sum.
This mistakenly assumes both extreme values equal the constant term \( [[d]] \).
This is off by two from the correct sum.
This is off by two from the correct sum.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]]x^{3} + ([[B]])x^{2} + ([[C]])x + ([[d]]) \). Applying the first derivative test to \( f'(x)=0 \), at what \( x \)-value does \( f \) have a local MAXIMUM?
solution: Solving \( f'(x) = 3([[a]])x^{2} + 2([[B]])x + [[C]] = 0 \) gives the two critical points \( x=[[r1]] \) and \( x=[[r2]] \). Since \( [[a]]>0 \), \( f \) increases then decreases through the smaller one, so the local maximum is at \( x=[[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the location of the local MINIMUM, not the maximum.
This adds the two critical points together instead of picking the smaller one.
This has the wrong sign.
This is the gap between the two critical points, not a critical point itself.
This is off by one from the correct location.
This is off by one from the correct location.

stem: Let \( f(x) = [[a]](x-[[r]])^{4} + [[b]](x-[[r]])^{2} + [[k]] \). Since \( f'(x) = 4([[a]])(x-[[r]])^{3} + 2([[b]])(x-[[r]]) \) has \( x=[[r]] \) as its only real zero (with \( [[a]] > 0 \) and \( [[b]] \ge 0 \)), \( f \) has a single global minimum there. What is the minimum value of \( f \)?
solution: At \( x=[[r]] \), both \( (x-[[r]])^{4} \) and \( (x-[[r]])^{2} \) vanish, so \( f([[r]]) = [[k]] \). Since every other term is non-negative, this is the global minimum value, \( [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the \( x \)-location of the minimum, not the minimum VALUE.
This mistakenly adds the location \( [[r]] \) to the minimum value.
This mistakenly adds the leading coefficients into the minimum value.
This has the wrong sign on the minimum value.
This is off by two from the correct minimum value.
This is off by two from the correct minimum value.
